title: EtherNet/IP I/O Implicit Messaging Anomaly
app: TA for Zeek (Splunkbase 5466), Corelight App for Splunk (Splunkbase 3884)
spl:
index=ot (sourcetype="zeek:cip_io:json" OR (sourcetype="zeek:enip:json" io_data=*))
| bin _time span=1s
| stats avg(data_length) as avg_len stdev(data_length) as sd_len count by _time connection_id source_h destination_h
| eventstats avg(avg_len) as baseline by connection_id
| where sd_len>0 OR abs(avg_len-baseline)>64
| table _time connection_id source_h destination_h avg_len sd_len baseline
description: Implicit I/O carries real-time control data; sudden changes in payload size or timing can signal cable issues, configuration drift, or injection attempts affecting deterministic control.
value: Implicit I/O carries real-time control data; sudden changes in payload size or timing can signal cable issues, configuration drift, or injection attempts affecting deterministic control.
implementation: Place Zeek on I/O scanner‚Äìadapter paths; prefer dedicated `cip_io` sourcetype when the TA splits logs. Learn normal `data_length` and sequence cadence per `connection_id`; alert on variance tied to control outages.
visualization: Line chart (data_length over time), Timeline (anomaly markers), Table (connection_id stats).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: TA for Zeek (Splunkbase 5466), Corelight App for Splunk (Splunkbase 3884).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype="zeek:enip:json"` (ICSNPP `cip_io.log` merged or `sourcetype="zeek:cip_io:json"`: `connection_id`, `data_length`, `sequence_number`, `io_data`).
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Place Zeek on I/O scanner‚Äìadapter paths; prefer dedicated `cip_io` sourcetype when the TA splits logs. Learn normal `data_length` and sequence cadence per `connection_id`; alert on variance tied to control outages.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot (sourcetype="zeek:cip_io:json" OR (sourcetype="zeek:enip:json" io_data=*))
| bin _time span=1s
| stats avg(data_length) as avg_len stdev(data_length) as sd_len count by _time connection_id source_h destination_h
| eventstats avg(avg_len) as baseline by connection_id
| where sd_len>0 OR abs(avg_len-baseline)>64
| table _time connection_id source_h destination_h avg_len sd_len baseline
```

Understanding this SPL

**EtherNet/IP I/O Implicit Messaging Anomaly** ‚Äî Implicit I/O carries real-time control data; sudden changes in payload size or timing can signal cable issues, configuration drift, or injection attempts affecting deterministic control.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="zeek:enip:json"` (ICSNPP `cip_io.log` merged or `sourcetype="zeek:cip_io:json"`: `connection_id`, `data_length`, `sequence_number`, `io_data`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): TA for Zeek (Splunkbase 5466), Corelight App for Splunk (Splunkbase 3884). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: zeek:cip_io:json, zeek:enip:json. Those sourcetypes align with what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="zeek:cip_io:json". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time connection_id source_h destination_h** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by connection_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where sd_len>0 OR abs(avg_len-baseline)>64` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Pipeline stage (see **EtherNet/IP I/O Implicit Messaging Anomaly**): table _time connection_id source_h destination_h avg_len sd_len baseline

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (data_length over time), Timeline (anomaly markers), Table (connection_id stats).
grandmaExplanation: Implicit I/O carries real-time control data; sudden changes in payload size or timing can signal cable issues, configuration drift, or injection attempts affecting deterministic control ‚Äî so faults, tampering, and safety issues are spotted right away.
